text:
Over the years, generative artificial intelligence (AI) has seen tremendous progress in image synthesis. However, when it comes to 3D medical imaging, generative models are not utilised as much. Almost all 2D image generation papers are relying heavily on diffusion probabilistic models. However, in medicine 3D volumetric scans, such as CT or MRI are predominantly used. Hence, there has been no systematic evaluation of 3D diffusion models yet. Synthetic data which is generated by 3D diffusion models can be useful for privacy preserving AI development and for data augmentation in rare diseases.

Abstract Recently, latent diffusion models achieved notable performance in generating medical images and 3D images. However, their high computational cost for each image remains a major problem. Moreover, the effect of latent diffusion models on 3D images remains unknown. In this paper, a new latent diffusion model is proposed to generate 3D medical images and reduce the computational cost of generated medical images. Specifically, the model uses the compressed latent space provided by Vector Quantized Generative Adversarial Network (VQ-GAN) to reduce the number of iterations required to output 3D medical images. The proposed model is evaluated using four medical datasets consisting of brain MRI, chest CT, breast MRI, and knee MRI. In the experiments, reader study is designed to qualitatively and quantitatively evaluate the output of the proposed model and examine whether the synthetic volumes that are analogous in appearance to clinical images can maintain the anatomical fidelity and maintain good cross-slice consistency while presenting more natural images.

Our experiments demonstrate that samples produced by our diffusion models are of high visual quality and diverse, and that the approach improves significantly upon GAN-based methods that are prone to mode collapse. Importantly, we show one viable clinical use of the synthetic samples: self-supervised pre-training on the synthetic data prior to downstream fine-tuning of the segmentation model on the limited patient dataset gives a marked improvement, especially for small numbers of samples. This yields up to 4 percentage points of improvement on Dice score for segmentation of tumors in breast MRI (from 0.91 to 0.95). Overall, our work suggests that latent diffusion models are highly suitable for synthesizing new, complex 3D medical volume images. This provides a valuable new way to advance medical imaging science where data are typically limited and there are substantial privacy constraints.
explanation: The text includes plausible, domain-specific details and concrete results, which an author could provide. However, a few awkward phrasings (like 'Abstract Recently' and long composite sentences) are characteristic of AI-generated text, suggesting a moderate likelihood of AI authorship.
